summary: My family’s journey to the United States is deeply connected to the Secret War. My parents were Hmong refugees who fled because of this conflict, which forced many like them to seek safety elsewhere. It’s because of the Secret War that my family ended up in America, starting a new life in Saint Paul, Minnesota. Their experiences as refugees have shaped my identity and the challenges I faced growing up.

The United States is where my family finally settled after fleeing the Secret War and refugee camps in Thailand. My parents arrived here in 1992, hoping for a better future, even though they had to give up education to work and support us. Growing up in America, I struggled with balancing my Hmong heritage and American identity, often feeling like I didn’t fully belong in either world. Despite these challenges, the opportunities here allowed me to attend college and find communities that supported me.

I was born and raised in Saint Paul, Minnesota, a place that has been home to my family since they arrived in the United States. Growing up there, I faced challenges in school, like being placed in ESL classes despite speaking perfect English. Saint Paul is where I first experienced the tension between my Hmong heritage and American culture, and it shaped much of my early life and identity. It’s also where my family built their new life after escaping the hardships of refugee camps.

Before coming to the United States, my parents lived in a refugee camp in Thailand, where they were taught to be agrarian farmers. Life there was difficult, and education was not an option for them. Their time in Thailand was a period of waiting and hardship before they could finally move to America in 1992. Knowing this history makes me appreciate the opportunities I have here, even though my parents had to sacrifice a lot.

I chose to attend the University of Minnesota Twin Cities somewhat on a whim, but it turned out to be one of the best decisions I made. There, I found communities like Tsev Hmoob and Students Today Leaders Forever that helped me embrace my Hmong heritage and find my voice. The university was where I grew academically and personally, eventually becoming the Treasurer of the Hmong Minnesota Student Association. It was a place that challenged me to step outside my comfort zone and connect with diverse groups of people.

Tsev Hmoob, or Hmong house, was my first community at the University of Minnesota Twin Cities. It was a living-learning community filled with peers who looked like me and shared similar ambitions for higher education. Being part of Tsev Hmoob helped me start accepting my Hmong heritage and provided a sense of belonging during my undergraduate years. It was a crucial foundation for my college experience and personal identity development.

Students Today Leaders Forever, or STLF, became my second community at college and felt like my Ohana. I went on a bus tour with them to Washington, D.C., where we stopped to do community service along the way. This experience pushed me to step out of my comfort zone and think deeply about what community means. The people I met through STLF opened my eyes to the diversity on campus and taught me how to support different communities.

Washington, D.C. was the destination of a bus tour I took with Students Today Leaders Forever during college. On the way there, we stopped for community service projects, which made the trip more meaningful. This journey challenged me to think about community and leadership in new ways and helped me connect with diverse groups of people beyond my immediate circles. The experience in D.C. was a pivotal moment in my college years.
